skill.name: react-19
skill.description: React 19 development guide for Actions, Server Components, new hooks, security patterns, and migration from React 18.
skill.summary: Build and migrate React 19 applications with practical patterns for forms, optimistic updates, Server Components, and security.
skill.author: Calel33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command, network, filesystem, script, and environment-access patterns, but the reviewed files are markdown guidance and examples rather than executable skill code. The findings are largely false positives from fenced React, shell, and security examples; residual risk is limited to users copying vulnerable examples that are explicitly marked as wrong or vulnerable.

low_findings.title: Copy-Paste Risk from Vulnerable Security Examples
low_findings.description: The security guide and migration guide include examples that intentionally show unsafe patterns such as module-scope secret access and unauthenticated Server Actions. They are clearly labelled as vulnerable or wrong, so this is not malicious behavior, but users may copy an unsafe example without the surrounding warning.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The risky code patterns are present in the referenced files, but the surrounding context explicitly labels them as vulnerable and provides corrected versions. This makes the marketplace risk moderate-to-low and dependent on user misuse.

low_findings.title: Static Command Execution Findings Are Markdown Examples
low_findings.description: The many external command detections are npm, npx, and audit commands shown in markdown installation and migration instructions. No executable script, hook, or runtime file was found that automatically runs these commands.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited locations are fenced bash examples in markdown documentation. The repository file list contains only markdown files and no executable implementation that invokes shell commands.

low_findings.title: Filesystem and Dynamic Import Findings Are Documentation False Positives
low_findings.description: The path traversal and dynamic import alerts come from normal React import examples and a Next.js configuration type annotation in markdown. They do not read files, traverse paths, or load arbitrary modules at runtime.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed text is documentation, and the flagged syntax is a relative source import plus a JSDoc type import. No runtime filesystem operation or dynamic user-controlled import was found.

low_findings.title: Network and Environment Access Examples Are Instructional
low_findings.description: Fetch calls and process.env usage appear in examples about keeping secrets server-side and avoiding client exposure. The examples are relevant to React Server Actions and do not indicate credential exfiltration by the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code examples use placeholder API URLs and environment variable names inside security guidance. There is no evidence of real secret collection or outbound transmission by marketplace skill code.

dangerous_patterns.title: Heuristic Combination Dismissed After Context Review
dangerous_patterns.description: The analyzer combined command, network, credential, and filesystem detections into a critical heuristic. Manual review found these signals spread across markdown examples for React installation, migration, and security education, not one executable data-exfiltration flow.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The dangerous combination exists only as separate documentation snippets. No evidence found of code that chains command execution, credential access, and network transmission.

content.value_statement: React 19 adds Actions, Server Components, and new hooks that change common application patterns. This skill gives practical guidance for building, securing, and migrating React 19 projects.
actual_capabilities: Explains React 19 Actions, Server Components, and execution boundaries.
Shows form handling patterns with useActionState and pending states.
Covers optimistic UI updates with useOptimistic and async actions.
Provides React 18 to React 19 migration steps and codemod guidance.
Documents security patterns for Server Actions, secrets, XSS, and data exposure.
Includes advanced examples for uploads, real-time interfaces, search, and Suspense.
limitations: It is a documentation skill and does not run migrations automatically.
Framework details may require verification against the target project version.
Examples use placeholder services and must be adapted before production use.
Security advice does not replace a project-specific security review.

use_cases.title: Upgrade a React 18 App
use_cases.description: Plan dependency updates, codemods, TypeScript fixes, and testing tasks for a React 19 migration.

use_cases.title: Build Server Action Forms
use_cases.description: Design forms that use Actions, validation, pending states, and secure server-side processing.

use_cases.title: Review React 19 Security Patterns
use_cases.description: Check Server Actions, secret handling, Client Component boundaries, and user input rendering risks.

prompt_templates.title: Explain React 19 Basics
prompt_templates.prompt: Explain the React 19 features I should understand first for a small application.

prompt_templates.title: Design an Action Form
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me design a React 19 form using useActionState, validation, pending state, and a secure Server Action.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a Migration
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a React 18 to React 19 migration plan for my app, including dependency updates, codemods, and tests.

prompt_templates.title: Audit Advanced Patterns
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my React 19 architecture for Server Component boundaries, optimistic updates, secret handling, and Suspense streaming risks.

output_examples.input: I need to migrate a dashboard from React 18 to React 19.
output_examples.output: A staged migration checklist covering package updates, codemods, TypeScript changes, removed APIs, and verification tests.

output_examples.input: I want a secure account deletion form with React 19 Actions.
output_examples.output: A design that includes authentication, authorization, validation, pending state, error handling, and safe server-side mutation flow.

output_examples.input: My optimistic comments UI feels inconsistent after failures.
output_examples.output: A review of optimistic state flow, rollback behavior, error display, and when to refresh server data.

best_practices: Start with the upgrade checklist before changing application code.
Keep secrets and privileged operations inside server-only code paths.
Validate user input before Server Actions mutate data or call APIs.
anti_patterns: Do not copy vulnerable examples without their corrected pattern.
Do not expose process.env values or API keys to Client Components.
Do not treat codemods as a replacement for tests and manual review.

faq.question: Does this skill execute code or install packages?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance and examples. Users decide whether to run any commands.

faq.question: Can it help with React 18 migrations?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes migration patterns, breaking changes, codemod guidance, and an upgrade checklist.

faq.question: Does it cover React Server Components?
faq.answer: Yes. It explains boundaries between Server Components, Client Components, and Server Actions.

faq.question: Does it include security guidance?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers Server Action authorization, secret handling, XSS prevention, and data exposure risks.

faq.question: Can it produce production-ready code?
faq.answer: It can guide production patterns, but examples need project-specific validation, testing, and dependency checks.

faq.question: Which AI tools can use this skill?
faq.answer: The report lists Claude, Codex, and Claude Code as supported tools.
